items.body_text:
[Marc Alex Forget]
Sure - no rush. Thanks!

---

[Adam Nelson]
Yeah, let's chat tomorrow about it

---

[Marc Alex Forget]
Hi Adam,

When we met at Ian's place, I heard you talk about Claude Enterprise Plan from Anthropic.

From what I heard it is an excellent tool for analyzing and summarizing meeting notes. 

This is something my team can benefit from. 

While I don't think it join live calls, if it shines at processing uploaded transcripts to extract actionable insights, generate summaries, and draft follow-up emails... will we be in a position to use a Teams feature to transcript and Claude to analyze and summarise meeting notes into the CRM (Salesforce Opp) and JIRA (GSE)?

---

[Adam Nelson]
Adam Nelson assuming they want VMware, yes.  However, there is a nuance to that answer.

Apt Cloud supports VMware Cloud Director natively, but with limited support.  It is a much more complicated implementation because it depends on a few different VMware components (VRops, vRealize, etc).  VMware is prone to issues in not being consistent with their usage data.  All to say, it is possible.  This is how MTC is exposed through Apt Cloud today.  We can expose self-service, but for MTC, we haven't exposed self-service to our customers (too many competing priorities).
CloudStack supports VMware as a hypervisor (at the vSphere API layer) out of the box and it is arguably the best supported hypervisor (from a feature perspective).  This means that the Aptum IaaS layer can support VMware clusters being orchestrated through that implementation.  We can dedicate clusters to customers within the Aptum IaaS layer if we need to.  So a customer would then be able to orchestrate shared resources (on our KVM infra) and also orchestrate their down dedicated VMware resources through the same interface.

items.subject: [SALES HELP] Sales Infrastructure & Cloud Questions
items.body_text:
[Marc Alex Forget]
Product, Marketing and Sales Enablement - Documents - Addendum & Order forms - All Documents

There is not an Order Form specific to colo. The Schedule ''A'' has to be updated to reflect colo services.

---

[Adam Nelson]
Does anyone know where the colo order form is?

 

Martin Tessier was looking

---

[Ian Rae]
It isn’t different however we have partners who are 100% Canadian controlled such as ThinkOn and Hypertec. This is a complex topic we probably should write an FAQ on this. We are able to provide Canadian controlled and operated solutions but not all of our solutions are 100% Canadian. Even if a company is Canadian controlled there is a spectrum of supply chain autonomy from foreign jurisdictions. If you need answers urgently just call me. Meantime we should get a FAQ for the website Marc Alex Forget something to add to the list of website TO Dos?

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So when the customer does directly ask, I don't feel comfortable with anything above. Can we get an official statement or something that I can refer customers to? This seems like a big enough deal that we should have an official stance / statement.

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Question I got from a prospective client:

 

How is that any different from Microsoft Canada? They are a Canadian entity with Canadian data centers, with us based ownership

---

[Martin Tessier]
I spoke to Melvin about that already.. It's a legal thing so we went to Sarah and here's her answer 

 

Jurisdiction:

If the contract is with our Canadian entity, it will generally be governed by Canadian law (federal or provincial, depending on the contract).
U.S. laws like the CLOUD Act technically only apply to U.S.-incorporated entities. A Canadian company is generally not directly subject, unless we have U.S.-based systems storing the data.

Data residency / access:

If the customer wants all data to stay in Canada, the clause should focus on:

Storage in Canadian servers.
Disclosure only as required under Canadian law.

Practical enforceability:

Aptum cannot promise to ignore legal obligations, but we can contractually promise notification and limited access.
Typical Canadian clauses include phrases like: “Data will not be transferred outside Canada without consent” or “Vendor will notify Customer of any government requests under Canadian law.”

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
I pinged Melvin

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Are we exempt from the cloud act

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
The cloud act is the primary concern

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So saying we meet regional data laws doesn't mean we are excluded from the US cloud act since we have an American parent company

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So does Microsoft.... Unless the us governments demands customer data from them.... Then it doesn't matter where it is... That's what people are worried about

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
I've shared that with a customer before

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
Aptum complies with all applicable data privacy regulations in the regions where we operate. For instance, in the EU, Aptum is in scope for GDPR as a data subprocessor-even though we don’t manage or access customer data directly. We demonstrate our compliance through our Data Processor Addendum, which outlines our obligations as a data subprocessor.

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Do we have anything official about our Canadian data sovereignty? I realise we are a Canadian company with Canadian data centers, but I've heard concerns more than once about our parent company being American

items.subject: Product delivery flow
items.body_text:
[Will Stevens]
Ok cool.  I was just making sure we were accounting for those details, which we clearly are.  Thanks team.

---

[Ben Kennedy]
None of this limits what we can do in Kirkland. In fact right now we don’t even have a single regular hosting rack. Once we repurpose MTC, at least we’ll have 2. I just don’t know the exact number that might be needed going forward

---

[George Revie]
Not an issue, there are racks planned for the deals you are referencing

---

[Will Stevens]
Or is this stuff "in prep for" better leveraging the space in Kirkland?

---

[Will Stevens]
I believe we have a couple deals in the works that are expecting to leverage the Kirkland DC (because it is a Canadian tier 3 facility). Does any of this limit the feasibility of that?

---

[Ben Kennedy]
This is more of an FYI for the group. But if anyone wants to weigh in, please do so. 

 

We need to remove our gear from the Kirkland MMRs. Not a big deal with no customers in Kirkland presently. While doing the necessary topology changes we were also going to do some other clean up. Specifically:

Remove network gear from PCI racks. There will be no PCI in Kirkland anymore. These were never setup as regular racks, so there are no Top-of-rack switches to repurpose. So at the moment these will be empty racks, network wise
Convert 2x MTC racks into regular 10G hosting racks. This will bring our count of (non-PCI) hosting racks with ready networking in Kirkland to: 2. I know there has been some more customer interest around Kirkland. But I haven't heard that there would need to be more than 2 racks sitting there waiting
After the topology changes are implemented our core network racks will remain as 1002.05 and 1002.06. But there will be space for any other internal gear, IE if shared storage is needed as part of some customer deal in Kirkland

---

[Martin Tessier]
Yep.. Aptum IaaS should take over..

---

[Will Stevens]
Again, we put our customers on ThinkOn's infra, but they are still our customers and we just changed where we are hosting our customer.  So I don't think it is technically a resell.  Anyway, we are getting down a rabbit hole.  All to say, we don't want to do the MTC route going forward.  We should be orienting around Aptum IaaS delivered through Apt Cloud as the ideal path (which supports both KVM and VMware at the hypervisor layer).

---

[Will Stevens]
I did a very high level review of what Cody was proposing for Technicost.  I asked him a bunch of questions and he was able to answer them.  I didn't do a full review of that RFP, but I did do a little bit of checking as a sniff test to make sure things were not totally off the rails, but I didn't fully vet or validate the solution being proposed.

---

[Martin Tessier]
Still .. our offering isn't What we use to call MTC.. our offering is a resale from TONS..

---

[Will Stevens]
We don't run MTC in our own DCs, but it is managed through Apt Cloud today on ThinkOn's infra.

---

[Will Stevens]
Thats not true.  It is exposed through Apt Cloud.  They are our customers (not thinkon's) even though they are running on ThinkOn's infra.  So we have to be careful because ThinkOn offers an MTC offering.  Our MTC offering for our customers depends on ThinkOn's offering.

---

[Adam Nelson]
It's for this: Technicost RFP - Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS).docx

---

[Martin Tessier]
what we use to call MTC doesn't exist anymore.. ..  it been migrated to ThinkOn MTC ( They have rebranded ThinkON VPC ) ..

---

[Will Stevens]
Martin Tessier is our MTC offering on top of ThinkOn MTC?

---

[Martin Tessier]
2 separate thing... ThinkOn provide our License for VMWAre ... and ThinkON MTC is a similar platform to what we are building with "Apt Cloud"  that run on VMWare..

---

[Adam Nelson]
I'm starting to think MA doesn't like Cody

---

[Adam Nelson]
There's apparently a call at noon today w/ Technicost and MA is not .... confident... in Cody's analysis of the situation

---

[Will Stevens]
I don't know if ThinkOn can provide us licenses to run in our own DCs.  Not sure

---

[Will Stevens]
I am not sure about the licensing piece

---

[Adam Nelson]
man, I feel so stupid

---

[Adam Nelson]
like get a license from thinkon for vmware vs. going w/ TO MTC?

---

[Adam Nelson]
ok, so we can provide vmware outside of thinkon's mtc?

---

[Will Stevens]
So the answer is yes, but it depends on how VMware is setup and managed, etc...

---

[Will Stevens]
Adam Nelson assuming they want VMware, yes.  However, there is a nuance to that answer.

Apt Cloud supports VMware Cloud Director natively, but with limited support.  It is a much more complicated implementation because it depends on a few different VMware components (VRops, vRealize, etc).  VMware is prone to issues in not being consistent with their usage data.  All to say, it is possible.  This is how MTC is exposed through Apt Cloud today.  We can expose self-service, but for MTC, we haven't exposed self-service to our customers (too many competing priorities).
CloudStack supports VMware as a hypervisor (at the vSphere API layer) out of the box and it is arguably the best supported hypervisor (from a feature perspective).  This means that the Aptum IaaS layer can support VMware clusters being orchestrated through that implementation.  We can dedicate clusters to customers within the Aptum IaaS layer if we need to.  So a customer would then be able to orchestrate shared resources (on our KVM infra) and also orchestrate their down dedicated VMware resources through the same interface.

---

[Adam Nelson]
The customer is w/ Leaseweb on a VMWare MTC

---

[Adam Nelson]
Martin Tessier Will Stevens - For technicost, are we able to use VMWare w/ Aptcloud? (I don't know what I'm talking about)

---

[Ben Kennedy]
Yup, makes sense

---

[Adam Nelson]
I'm trying to figure out (distilled to as simple as i can think of it)... "what did we buy that we can sell, what of that did we sell, what's left?" on the PS side it's people & hours, on compute side it's servers, on colo side it's space & power

---

[Will Stevens]
The other two are SCADACore and Alberta New Home Warranty Program

---

[Will Stevens]
ES Williams is 5 of the 7, but as separate customers.

---

[Ben Kennedy]
We have X capacity in connectivity (bandwidth, cross-connects, transit/peering) and infrastructure devices (switches, firewalls, load balancers)
We've sold/deployed Y to customers and current operations
We have Z capacity available to sell or deploy without spending more

comments from me: I'd say this is mostly right. But for x and z, I mean it would be like x1, x2, etc and z1, z2, etc. Given the number of different capacity elements.

Firewalls and load balancers are tricky for X. We have inventory of firewalls, so you could call that X capacity. For load balancers we are at Citrix's whims. Traditionally it's an incremental cost, not a fixed cost for X capacity. This year we are use a loophole to only pay for 5 licenses, 1 of which is licensing all our managed hosting load balancers as that license gives as access to a 1TB pool. So this year we could call that "x" capacity

---

[Ben Kennedy]
"do capitalized hours happen in non-project specific contexts?" - For me team, no

 

" To me it would make sense that if work is being capitalized, then a dedicated jira project would be setup to do that work.  Is that a fair assumption?" - As long as we can create some link between the jira project used for the "regular" work and the jira project used for the "capitalized" work, then this could work

---

[Adam Nelson]
I mean, isn't ES Williams technically 1? or are we counting them as 7

---

[Adam Nelson]
oof,,, I wish you didn't take screenshots haha

items.body_text:
[Ian Rae]
LMK if this makes any sense.

---

[Ian Rae]
3) GSE ideally would be focused on custom/complex

---

[Ian Rae]
2) I would like sales focused on selling the lego kits (private cloud OR public cloud variations)

---

[Ian Rae]
1) I do think service delivery has to be able to handle supporting the lego blocks of an existing kit including supplying extra blocks. Without sales needing to be involved.

---

[Ian Rae]
Yup accurate although a bit detailed for the current audience but very good. So here is what I think we probably need to align around: https://aptum.com/services/ and this: https://aptum.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/Product/overview - if our public facing and internal facing docs don't align that is an issue. Also we need to get ahead in the GSE process of (a) I need lego blocks to add to my existing kit (b) I need a new lego kit (c) I want a lego "city" (custom or multiple integrated kits).

---

[Adam Nelson]
3. Product Definitions
3.1 Apt Cloud (The Platform)

"Apt" conveys capability, precision, and readiness — a cloud that is tailored to needs, not one-size-fits-all. Apt Cloud ties directly to Aptum's identity as a hybrid cloud managed services provider.

Apt Cloud is Aptum's next-generation cloud operations platform, accessed at portal.aptum.com. It is a white-labeled instance of CloudOps Software that acts as the single control plane for Aptum-delivered cloud services.

What it delivers:

Self-service provisioning (VMs, networks, storage, DNS)
Role-based access control (RBAC) with granular permissions
Cost and usage visibility with real-time cost estimator
Lifecycle management (create, scale, snapshot, decommission)
Activity logging (visible activity logs across all services; note: not traditional monitoring/push alerts at this layer today)
Unified governance across private and hyperscale clouds
Integration with Aptum managed services workflows
Multi-tenant organizations with white-label branding (per-reseller)
Full REST API (API-first design)
Monetization engine: Product catalogs, pricing, commitments, utility pricing, revenue reporting, invoicing, discounts, credits, CC integration, tax integration (bold = already set up; italic = available, could be using)
Link to support / ticketing system (planned)

What it is NOT:

Apt Cloud does NOT provide physical infrastructure
Apt Cloud does NOT replace hyperscale provider consoles (Azure Portal, AWS Console)
Apt Cloud does NOT bypass managed services engagement

Services available within Apt Cloud (portal.aptum.com) today:

Aptum IaaS — VMs on CloudStack (new)
MTC — VMs on ThinkOn / VMware Cloud Director (legacy MTC customers)
Microsoft Azure — Instances, disks, networks, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
Cloudflare DNS — Domains and DNS records (replaces SuperDNS; built and plan developed, rollout pending customer communication)
Available to adopt (functionality built, not yet configured): AWS, GCP, Kubernetes (standalone)
3.2 Aptum IaaS (The Infrastructure)

Aptum IaaS consists of compute, storage, and networking services running on Aptum-owned hardware in Aptum data centres, orchestrated by Apache CloudStack. It is delivered in two models:

Virtual Private Cloud (VPC):

Multi-tenant shared compute infrastructure
Logical isolation with private networking (VLANs; VXLAN support TBD — needs validation with KVM implementation)
Dedicated resource quotas
Predictable performance within a pooled system

Private Cloud:

Single-tenant dedicated compute nodes
Enhanced security, compliance, and customization
Custom networking and storage configurations
Same operational model and automation layer as VPC
Combines technical infrastructure with managed services (people + technology)
Note: Bare Metal as a Service (BMaaS) integration is not yet implemented. The platform has a 5c/Hypertec BMaaS integration (Canonical MaaS at the CloudStack layer, built by CloudOps Inc) which could be leveraged. Currently, "Private Cloud" means dedicated physical hosts delivered through the virtual orchestration layer — not direct bare metal provisioning.

---

[Ian Rae]
We will be in the basement

---

[Ian Rae]
Hi popping out to get some stuff. Entrance is next to garage door in driveway if you get here early door is open .
